issue_title: Default cookie secret - inconsistent behaviour
issue_body:
The help string for `NotebookApp.cookie_secret` says:

> By default this is a new random number every time you start the Notebook.

This is wrong, because we store the cookie secret in `NotebookApp.cookie_secret_file`, which defaults to a file in the *runtime* directory. The actual behaviour is hard to describe because it depends on your platform:

- On Linux, or other platforms using XDG_RUNTIME_DIR, the secret will be shared by all servers until you log off or restart your computer.
- On other platforms, the runtime directory is not automatically cleared, so the cookie secret is fully persistent.

Persisting the cookie secret is convenient because authentication cookies stored in your browser remain valid. Generating a new secret for each server is obviously more secure.

- [ ] Decide what we're doing by default
- [ ] Make it consistent across platforms
- [ ] Describe it accurately in the help strings, along with how to change it.

issue_title: Dangerous performance issue with ModelConversionError in nested models
issue_body:
There is a very old change by @ryanolson in current master (1.1.1) which may cause critical performance issues when a ModelConversionError occurs in a deeply nested model. **This issue may cause the process to get stuck for a very long time and may be misused to starve workers on an application.**

https://github.com/schematics/schematics/blob/master/schematics/transforms.py#L108
https://github.com/schematics/schematics/blob/master/schematics/transforms.py#L415

The pattern used on both places seems to be designed to catch TypeError occurring when the called function/method doesn't expect the `mapping` kwarg, in order to call it without it. I suppose this was made to avoid adding the `mapping` kwarg to all related defs. But it has a huge issue - it is too broad and also catches ModelConversionError and possibly other errors. When a conversion error is caught, the call is retried without the mapping argument, converting the entire model again. Since two such `except`s are encountered for each level of nested models, the resulting complexity is **2^(2*N), where N is the depth of a model at which the conversion error occurred**. The application I'm working on can easily reach 10 levels of model nesting or more, so a conversion error there may be raised over a million times!

I added a pull request with a test that fails while this bug is still present:
https://github.com/schematics/schematics/pull/471/files
Note that if the `assert` is removed, the test might run for months if not killed...

An obvious fix is changing the `Exception` on these two places to a `TypeError`, but a `TypeError` could be caused due to some other reason. A better solution is adding the `mapping` kwarg everywhere it needs to be, but there may be compatibility issues with custom schematics extension (like an application I'm working on has, I went with this solution anyway). If it is deemed to be the best solution, I'll make a PR for that too.
(https://github.com/petee-d/schematics/pull/1/files)

issue_title: Work with read receipts
issue_body: See https://matrix.org/docs/spec/client_server/r0.3.0.html#id57 for documentation. Consider implementing so that doing anything with a particular event automatically marks it as read (would only be desirable for bots, so implementation would have to account for that somehow).

issue_title: Loading Screen Observation
issue_body:
Could we add some of the following details to the observations? These details are visible to a human player on the loading screen:

1. The map (name, version, anything required to get a unique ID in case the maps change over time)
2. Map spawn locations (although maybe this could be included in the map details in point 1)
3. Each player's chosen race (T, V, Z, random) - not the actual race for random

Since a player's strategy will often depend on the map, the opponent's race and their location, all of these details would help a bot in the early game.

A more advanced bot may study the feature layers, but simpler bots could achieve a lot with less effort if these details were available.

issue_title: Encrypting and pycryptodome
issue_body:
I've been running into an issue in an environment where I have [pycryptodome](https://github.com/Legrandin/pycryptodome) installed (dependency of another package) when I try to encrypt passwords in `.girafferc`:

```
± giraffez config --init
colorful: true
connections:
  db1:
    host: null
    password: null
    username: null
  default: db1

Successfully created file '/Users/username/.girafferc'
Set the username and password using the config module:

    giraffez config --set connections.db1.host <host>
    giraffez config --set connections.db1.username <username>
    giraffez config --set connections.db1.password <password>
Key file '/Users/username/.giraffepg' created successfully.


± giraffez config --set connections.db1.password some_pass
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/bin/giraffez", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/giraffez/__main__.py", line 25, in main
    MainCommand().run()
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/giraffez/core.py", line 80, in run
    args.run(args)
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/giraffez/commandline.py", line 177, in run
    c.set_value(key, value)
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/giraffez/config.py", line 298, in set_value
    value = self.encrypt(value, path)
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/giraffez/config.py", line 171, in encrypt
    value = "ENCRYPTED:{}".format(ensure_str(self.cipher.encrypt(value)))
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/giraffez/encrypt.py", line 64, in encrypt
    return base64.b64encode(iv + cipher.encrypt(raw))
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/Crypto/Cipher/_mode_cbc.py", line 160, in encrypt
    expect_byte_string(plaintext)
  File "/Users/username/miniconda3/envs/env/lib/python3.6/site-packages/Crypto/Util/_raw_api.py", line 194, in expect_byte_string
    raise TypeError("Only byte strings can be passed to C code")
TypeError: Only byte strings can be passed to C code
```

I *think* that if we change [the line here](https://github.com/capitalone/giraffez/blob/master/giraffez/encrypt.py#L61) from `raw = self.pad(raw)` to `raw = self.pad(raw).encode("utf-8")` that would fix the issue.  In the `decrypt` function it's already assumed that the string is utf-8 encoded, so I don't think this would introduce any downstream issues.  That being said I've only done a little bit of testing.

From some reading it sounds like pycryptodome is more supported than pycrypto going forward, but I'm not familiar with the arguments there.

issue_title: picard AddOrReplaceReadGroups fails unpredictibly
issue_body:
For whatever reason, when using [Picard](https://github.com/BD2KGenomics/cgl-docker-lib/tree/master/picardtools) to attach ReadGroups to our aligned files, this occasionally fails and instead of writing out the same BAM file as we started with, but with the new read group attached, it writes out a 30KB corrupt BAM file. This is obviously disappointing.

- [x] @fnothaft will WAR this using `bwa-kit`, which is actually what we should do anyways because rewriting a 150GB BAM file to add ~30B of ASCII text to the header is wasteful. (see #153)
- [ ] @almussel will debug _why_ this is happening with Picard? You can repro it by running the batch alignment pipeline on `LP6005441-DNA_A12` from the SGD project. (this issue)
- [ ] We should fix the garbage we're writing in the platform fields.
- [ ] We should close the gaps between the bwa and bwakit pipelines (see #154)

issue_title: Extracted files seem to be corrupted
issue_body:
Hi,

I have written (mostly copied) a python script ([br.txt](https://github.com/log2timeline/dfvfs/files/1337735/br.txt)) using dfvfs to automate the extraction of SOFTWARE registry hive from a forensic image (including volume shadow copies). However, the hive extracted differs in hash (MD5) from the ones extracted using X-Ways and AD FTK Imager.
```
e.g. cfreds_2015_data_leakage_pc.E01
br.py (using dfvfs)    54449C66393659C1EB5BD98D74AD5E5D
X-Ways                 DE50BC9B74A65373B540796D9FFD9D1F
AD FTK                 DE50BC9B74A65373B540796D9FFD9D1F
```
![binary comparison](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2588284/30922294-4ba6b13a-a3db-11e7-93d0-6f29bd69b549.JPG)

I have tried using different forensic images, latest 2 versions of dfvfs, python 2 & 3, but I still encounter this issue.

Steps to reproduce the issue:

1. Rename br.txt to br.py
2. Download cfreds pc image (4x e01 files) from https://www.cfreds.nist.gov/data_leakage_case/data-leakage-case.html
3. Create output directory
4. `python br.py <downloaded cfreds image> <output directory>`

Would appreciate your assistance in resolving the issue, pls.

Regards.

issue_title: Research Host.puppet_ca_proxy
issue_body:
The semantics of the `Host.puppet_ca_proxy` field are not well understood. As a result, the field is omitted. The API documentation mentions it, though. From the API documentation:

> POST /api/hosts
> host[puppet_ca_proxy_id]
> Value: Must be a number.

Research the semantics of this field and add it to `nailgun.entities` if appropriate.

issue_title: adding agents in a poisson fashion
issue_body:
Hi All, 

I would like to add agents (without any positional information) on step basis (time) based on average arrival rate in a poisson fashion. These agent will have a certain randomly chosen stay time. This randomly chosen stay time will be alloctaed to every agent when it is a added into the simulation. These agents will do some stuff and once the stay time of an agent is over, agent will be removed. There will also be a maximum number of agents in the simulations and in case, if maximum agents are already present in the simulation at a certain time, the next new agent should wait until a previous one is removed. 

I was wondering if there is an easy way of doing this Mesa or any example that can give me a similar idea? Thanks in advance.

issue_title: BigIq RegKey Device licensing API is broken
issue_body:
The following  code fails:
```
from f5.bigiq import ManagementRoot as bigiq_management_root

bigiq = bigiq_management_root(host, user, password) 
bigiq.cm.device.licensing.pool.regkey.licenses_s.get_collection()
licenses = bigiq.client.cm.device.licensing.pool.regkey.licenses_s.get_collection()
k, = licenses[0].offerings_s.get_collection()
k.members_s.get_collection()
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/gabdav01/.virtualenvs/splatt/lib/python3.5/site-packages/f5/bigip/mixins.py", line 102, in __getattr__
    raise AttributeError(error_message)
AttributeError: '<class 'f5.bigiq.cm.device.licensing.pool.regkey.licenses.Offering'>' object has no attribute 'members_s'
'<class 'f5.bigiq.cm.device.licensing.pool.regkey.licenses.Offering'>' object has no attribute 'members_s'
```
This is likely to be caused by missing 
```
        self._meta_data['allowed_lazy_attributes'] = [
            Members_s
        ]
```
in
https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/blob/development/f5/bigiq/cm/device/licensing/pool/regkey/licenses.py#L69

The reference iControl REST endpoint:
```
/mgmt/cm/device/licensing/pool/regkey/licenses/{id}/offerings/{key}/members
```

Essentially, I am just sending a PUT request to update the quantity of a line item as explained in the example usage. However, after updating the line item quantity, discounts are not recalculated and in the response price_excl_tax and price_excl_tax_excl_discounts are equal. Only when I request the whole basket again, the line items are updated.

issue_title: API diffs not showing readonly ref as readonly
issue_body:
It looks like the API diff generator not aware of readonly ref, so it reads as though you're adding APIs that return a writable ref to ImmutableArray:

https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/release-notes/2.1/api-diff/2.0-vs-2.1_System.Collections.Immutable.md#systemcollectionsimmutable

Differentiating `readonly ref` from `ref` seems like it could be helpful to folks like me.
@karelz suggested filing the issue until @weshaggard is available.

issue_title: Deprecated 'Order allow,deny' syntax for Apache 2.4
issue_body:
The config generated by letsencrypt contains these stanzas:

```
# allow all
       Order allow,deny
       allow from all

# deny all
      Order allow,deny
      Deny from all
```

These are not valid when running Apache 2.4. Instead they need to be:

```
# allow all 
      Require all granted    

# deny all
      Require all denied
```

issue_title: Webroot Plugin: Cleanup Error
issue_body:
Hi! I'm using the letsencrypt client from a docker container with the following command:
`letsencrypt-auto certonly --agree-tos -m email@domain.com -d "domain.com" --webroot --webroot-path=/srv/www/letsencrypt`

The command fails with this error: 
`An unexpected error occurred:
OSError: [Errno 16] Device or resource busy: '/srv/www/letsencrypt/.well-known/acme-challenge'
Please see the logfiles in /var/log/letsencrypt for more details.`

The log file shows the exception that has been thrown here:
`2016-05-01 06:02:09,853:DEBUG:letsencrypt.main:Exiting abnormally:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/bin/letsencrypt", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/main.py", line 692, in main
    return config.func(config, plugins)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/main.py", line 509, in obtain_cert
    _, action = _auth_from_domains(le_client, config, domains, lineage)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/main.py", line 93, in _auth_from_domains
    lineage = le_client.obtain_and_enroll_certificate(domains)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/client.py", line 274, in obtain_and_enroll_certificate
    certr, chain, key, _ = self.obtain_certificate(domains)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/client.py", line 246, in obtain_certificate
    self.config.allow_subset_of_names)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/auth_handler.py", line 74, in get_authorizations
    self._respond(resp, best_effort)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/auth_handler.py", line 134, in _respond
    self._cleanup_challenges(active_achalls)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/auth_handler.py", line 266, in _cleanup_challenges
    self.auth.cleanup(achalls)
  File "/root/.local/share/letsencrypt/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/plugins/webroot.py", line 234, in cleanup
    os.rmdir(root_path)
OSError: [Errno 16] Device or resource busy: '/srv/www/letsencrypt/.well-known/acme-challenge'`

Apparently it's related to the cleanup process. The interesting thing is that the text-file used for authorization actually is being deleted although the error implies otherwise. I commented out the following line:
https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/blob/master/certbot/plugins/webroot.py#L234

Hi, thank you for opening an issue! We really appreciate you taking the time to give us feedback.

issue_title: metadata.search returns keys in new-to-old order
issue_body:
```
import json
from theworld.image import imagery
import descarteslabs as dl
dlk = dl.raster.dlkey_from_latlon(31.42417852, -96.24699805, 30., 512, 0)

# get scenes using imagery.search
scenes1 = imagery.search(satellite='L8', shape=json.dumps(dlk['geometry']), start_time='2014-01-01', end_time='2014-03-01')

# get scenes using metadata.search
scenes2 = dl.metadata.search(const_id=['L8'], geom=json.dumps(dlk['geometry']), start_time='2014-01-01', end_time='2014-03-01')
```

`imagery.search` returns keys in old-to-new order.
```
[u'meta_LC80260382014015_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014031_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014047_v1']
```


`metadata.search` returns keys in new-to-old order.
```
[u'meta_LC80260382014047_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014031_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014015_v1']
```

IMO, old-to-new order is preferable, because then `.raster(multiple_scenes)` will make a mosaic image with the most recent imagery on top.

Now, things get more complicated when you pass multiple `const_id`s to `metadata.search`.  The results come back grouped by `const_id`, and are sorted new-to-old within each `const_id`.  For example, searching L7 and L8 yields:

```
[u'meta_LE70260382014055_v1',
 u'meta_LE70260382014039_v1',
 u'meta_LE70260382014023_v1',
 u'meta_LE70260382014007_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014047_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014031_v1',
 u'meta_LC80260382014015_v1']
```

I don't know how difficult this would be to implement, but I propose that we return the scenes with a global ordering based explicitly on the scene acquisition time, going from old-to-new.  In that case, `.raster(multiple_scenes)` would make a mosaic image with the most recent imagery on top.

issue_title: post_load not running with fields.Method schemas
Deprecate the Select field
issue_body:
I propose to deprecate the `Select` field in favor of a `OneOf` validator. Reasons:

* The `Select` field is just a validator masked as a field. It doesn't perform any real (de)serialization.
* It works only with basic data types. The field's `choices` argument can't be a list of "complex" objects (e.g. dates).
* Currently there is no built-in way to validate a field against a fixed set of values.
* A convenience method to associate choices to labels (e.g. to populate a form select field) would be quite useful. The validator could implement it in a generic way (i.e. not tied to any data type in particular).

Please let me know your opinion.

issue_title: proposal: add fields.Enum
issue_body:
Although marshmallow is not just for sqlalchemy, I think a reasonable standard for completeness is: "It can be used to serialize the basic types of sqlalchemy." One thing that's kinda inconvenient right now is enums. My current issue is with sqlalchemy columns constructed from pep 435 enums, although it applies equally to the enums themselves.

so I propose adding a new kind* of field, `Enum`, which would allow some syntax like

0. `fields.Enum(int)`
1. `fields.Enum(fields.String)`
2. `fields.Enum(MyEnumClass)`

it wouldn't really have to do much except fetch via attrname.name or attrname.value** instead of just attrname by default, and, if the enum class is available, autogenerate a OneOf validator expr. It should also appear from the outside (e.g. to consumers like apispec) to be of whatever type the actual value is (i.e. the type of the name or the type of the value, depending).

I've never made a custom field before & don't really know how it's done so it would be awesome if somebody more skilled could do this for me, but I have a feeling I'll have to submit my own PR after figuring it all out <_curses under breath_>

*Haskell nerds: I use the term 'kind' advisedly

**Perhaps by-name & by-value would be 2 separate kinds

issue_title: Allow use of dataclass from python 3.7
issue_body:
Dataclasses are a new standard library feature to allow for less boilerplate in object oriented code coming in python 3.7. 

* [A short example](https://blog.jetbrains.com/pycharm/2018/04/python-37-introducing-data-class/)
* [PEP](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0557/)
* [current documentation](https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/dataclasses.html?highlight=dataclass#module-dataclasses)

For sacred, the only problem seems to be that since the `__init__` of a class is no longer explicitely defined, we cannot decorate it.
This is easily fixed though:

```
#! /usr/bin/env python

from dataclasses import dataclass
from sacred import Experiment

# this could easily be moved into the Ingredient class
def capture_init(ing):
  def decorator(cls):
    cls.__init__ = ing.capture(cls.__init__)
    return cls
  return decorator


ex = Experiment('dataclasstest')

@ex.config
def conf():
  factor = 2
  x = 2
  y = 3

@capture_init(ex)
@dataclass
class Vector2D:
  x: float
  y: float

  @ex.capture
  def scale(self, factor):
    print((self.x*factor, self.y*factor))

@ex.automain
def main():
  v = Vector2D()
  v.scale()
```

This results in the expected result:

```bash
(py37env) dwynen@taurus /t* $ python test.py -u
INFO - dataclasstest - Running command 'main'
INFO - dataclasstest - Started
(4, 6)
INFO - dataclasstest - Completed after 0:00:00
```

So *if* we want to support dataclasses (they are python>=3.7, so this is not a given) then it would be easy to add a decorator the the `Ingredient` class or to modify `@ing.capture`.

Thoughts? Opinions? :)

issue_title: Underflow checking of signed multiplication
issue_body:
@NikolajBjorner This is not strictly about Z3, but about the following paper:

   https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/wp-content/uploads/2016/02/z3prefix.pdf

(I believe some of the code in Z3 regarding overflow-checking is based on this paper, so perhaps it's related in that way.)

On page 12 when it talks about checking underflow for signed multiplication, I think we need to make an explicit check that neither `x` nor `y` equals `0`.

For instance, if `x` is `0`, and `y` is any negative number, then the hypothesis that the signs are different holds (essentially `0` being identified as sign-bit `0`), but the consequent fails since the extended multiplication produces `0`; and thus the check that all the initial `N+1` bits must be `1` fails. So, a straightforward reading of the formula will  incorrectly classify this case as an underflow.

A minor point perhaps, but wanted to double check with you that this is indeed needed. Or is there some other language in the paper that I missed that already covers this case?

Removing Z3_get_error_msg_ex breaks backwards compatibility
issue_body:
I develop unofficial Haskell bindings for Z3's API and one my users have noticed that you have removed ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex```. This change was made by commit bea68cd194b0 ("remove deprecated API functionality").

I know that ```Z3_get_error_msg``` has been deprecated for some time, and consequently I replaced it with ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex```. Removing ```Z3_get_error_msg``` it's fine, or even making it a synonym of ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex```. But removing ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex``` breaks backwards compatibility of Z3 4.4.2 with respect to 4.4.1, which is not nice. One would like to assume that any 4.4.y version will be backwards compatible with 4.4.x for y > x.

Could you instead deprecate ```Z3_get_error_msg_ex``` and remove it in Z3 4.5 or later?

Thanks,

issue_title: Floating-point conversions started returning "unknown"
issue_body:
@wintersteiger 

Hi Christoph,

I noticed you made some changes to the floating-point theory recently, squashing some bugs. Unfortunately, certain benchmarks that Z3 were able to solve before now started returning `unknown`.

Here's one example:

```
(set-logic ALL)
(define-fun s3 () Int 0)
(define-fun s1 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53) ((_ to_fp 11 53) roundNearestTiesToEven (/ 1.0 2.0)))
(define-fun s8 () RoundingMode roundNearestTiesToEven)
(declare-fun s0 () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53)) ; tracks user variable "x"
(define-fun s2 () Bool (fp.eq s0 s1))
(define-fun s4 () Bool (fp.isNaN s0))
(define-fun s5 () Bool (fp.isInfinite s0))
(define-fun s6 () Bool (or s4 s5))
(define-fun s7 () Bool (not s6))
(define-fun s9 () Int (to_int (fp.to_real s0)))
(define-fun s10 () Int (ite s7 s9 s3))
(define-fun s11 () Bool (= s3 s10))
(assert s2)

(check-sat)
```

These constraints are satisfiable, and Z3 was able to find a model for this before.  (In fact `s0` being `0` satisfies it trivially.) But now with the latest build out of github, I'm getting an `unknown`.

issue_title: handle options --verbose and --silent correctly
issue_body:
Related to #50 

The correct combination of these two should be as follows:

|--verbose  |  --silent | desired behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
|absent (default is False) | absent (default is False) | all messages except those printed by info |
|absent (default is False) | present (True) | no messages printed
| present (True) | absent (default is False) | all messages, including info are printed
| present (True) |  present (True) | error |

Current behavior is:

|--verbose  |  --silent | current behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
|absent (default is False) | absent (default is False) | all messages except those printed by info |
|absent (default is False) | present (ignored, so the default - False) | all messages except those printed by info |
| present (True) | absent (default is False) | all messages, including info are printed
| present (True) |  present (ignored, so the default - False) | all messages, including info are printed |

issue_title: grad_clipping clips gate activations, not gradient
issue_body:
I don't understand the way grad_clipping is implemented in the recurrent layers. Instead of clipping gradients, gate activations get clipped. Is there a reason for this? 1. I don't know any publication doing this and 2. it doesn't solve the exploding gradient problem.

The correct implementation should clip the gradient and it therefore should be implemented in the update functions.

Also you use theano.gradient.grad_clip, which works on each parameter independently (= hard clipping). Consider limiting the L2 norm of the gradient to a certain value, dividing each parameter by the same value and thus preserving the correct directiong of the gradient.
See [On the difficulty of training Recurrent Neural Networks](http://www.jmlr.org/proceedings/papers/v28/pascanu13.pdf), Algorithm 1. 

I use it the following way: 
(copied from https://github.com/kastnerkyle/minet/blob/master/minet/net.py#L236 )
```
def sgd_with_grad_clipping(loss_or_grads, params, learning_rate, rescale):
    grads = lasagne.updates.get_or_compute_grads(loss_or_grads, params)
    updates = OrderedDict()

    grad_norm = T.sqrt(sum(map(lambda x: T.sqr(x).sum(), grads)))
    not_finite = T.or_(T.isnan(grad_norm), T.isinf(grad_norm))
    grad_norm = T.sqrt(grad_norm)
    scaling_num = rescale
    scaling_den = T.maximum(rescale, grad_norm)
    for n, (param, grad) in enumerate(zip(params, grads)):
        grad = T.switch(not_finite, 0.1 * param,
            grad * (scaling_num / scaling_den))
        updates[param] = param - learning_rate * grad
    return updates   
```

issue_title: Make get_params optionally return expressions?
issue_body:
While implementing a form of [BinaryConnect](https://github.com/MatthieuCourbariaux/BinaryConnect) I noticed that get_params returns only the shared variables, but not expressions. That makes perfect sense e.g. for low-rank dense layers (where W = UV is a Theano expression with U and V as shared variables). I now have the opposite use case: I'd like to register an expression as a parameter of which I later want to compute the gradient, so I want get_param to return that expression instead of the shared variables contained. In principle that is easy to implement: one could add a keyword "only_shared=True" in get_params and modify the return statement. 

If you like the idea I can open a pull request.

[Further info on use case]: My use case is as follows: I have a parameter that is binary (e.g. 0 or 1), but I'd still like to use SGD to optimize that parameter. To this end I follow BinaryConnect and define a new real-valued hidden variable *p* with support [0,1] (through clipping). The binary parameter is then implemented as a simple Bernoulli process with probability *p*. The parameter *p* is then updated in the direction of the gradient with respect to the binary parameter (so e.g. if the binary parameter b was 1, and the is positive with respect to this parameter, then p is increased).

issue_title: Where should pynwb validate schema relationships?
issue_body:
I'm currently working on getting a bunch of calcium imaging data into pynwb. I've got additional behavior data streams and stimulus streams, but currently, I'm just trying to build out a minimal file with calcium traces.

My short term problem is that I have no clean way to check whether I'm writing files correctly.

But I feel like this problem highlights broader issues with when and where pynwb enforces the schema & I'm hoping this issue can open a discussion about how we might be able to improve pynwb for users.

To start, pynwb won't let me mock up a single object without having it's dependencies specified. Practically speaking, this means I have to build out a script to write data backwards... I want to save calcium traces, but I can't create the RoiResponseSeries until I've created an ImageSegmentation, so I try to make a ImageSegmentation, but pynwb won't let me until I've created a PlaneSegmentation, etc, etc, etc.

now I've got dozens of objects in memory (6+n, where 'n' is the number of neurons in my recording) and I want to save them which means I need to register them with the NWBFile container.

This happens by creating a module and running "add_container" over and over again.

``` Python
# now let's create the processing module to store this in
ophys_module = nwb_file.create_processing_module(
    name="ophys",
    description="calcium responses",
    source="my rig",
)

ophys_module.add_container(dFF_wrapper)
ophys_module.add_container(dFF_series)
ophys_module.add_container(seg_interface)
ophys_module.add_container(segmentation)
ophys_module.add_container(imaging_plane)
for roi in roi_list:
    ophys_module.add_container(roi)
ophys_module.add_container(max_proj)
ophys_module.add_container(optical_channel)
```

Note: at this point, pynwb does absolutely zero validation or inspection of the chain of dependencies. This means that if I have a bug and lose track of the set of required objects and they don't get passed into the `add_container` method on the processing module object, **pynwb lets me save an invalid file**

https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/issues/146

It should be impossible to save an invalid file. I need to be able to trust that every file I save with pynwb is valid and can be read back out.

And while this is true, I have no quick way to check whether or not this is true because "validate.py" is broken:

https://github.com/NeurodataWithoutBorders/pynwb/issues/127

While fixing these bugs will go a long way toward giving users some peace of mind that they will be able to read their data back out, 

## Where should pynwb validate the data model? 

- If on object creation (as it is now), can pynwb take advantage of this when registering objects with a module by inspecting the relationships, traversing the tree, and adding all of the relevant objects instead of making the user call `add_container` over and over?
- Alternatively, if we want pynwb to do another round of validation when objects get registered with NWBFile, can pynwb be a bit lighter on validation when creating objects? For example, letting the user build the objects they need without specifying the relationships, then register them with one another later & validate against the schema when they get added to the NWBFile?

issue_title: Problems with object.provides handling in PE backend
issue_body:
I was trying to load a PE binary, from a Borland C++/Delphi project, with `auto_load_libs` True.

It gives the following error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "wpl-angr3.py", line 46, in <module>
    proj = angr.Project(sample, load_options={'auto_load_libs':True})#, 'main_opts': {'custom_base_addr': 0x0}})
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/angr/project.py", line 107, in __init__
    self.loader = cle.Loader(self.filename, **load_options)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/cle-4.6.5.27-py2.7.egg/cle/loader.py", line 101, in __init__
    self._load_dependencies()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/cle-4.6.5.27-py2.7.egg/cle/loader.py", line 203, in _load_dependencies
    self.add_object(obj, base_addr)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/cle-4.6.5.27-py2.7.egg/cle/loader.py", line 341, in add_object
    self.memory.add_backer(base_addr, obj.memory)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/cle-4.6.5.27-py2.7.egg/cle/memory.py", line 36, in add_backer
    raise ValueError("Address %#x is already backed!" % start)
ValueError: Address 0x40000000 is already backed!
```

I've tried to track down the problem, and its caused because of this code 

```
        if self.binary is not None and self.binary.endswith('.dll'):
            self.provides = os.path.basename(self.binary)
        else:
            self.provides = None
```

The problem is, that Borland C++ calls its `dll`-s `bpl` and so `object.provides` will be `None`, so `cle` will try to load the same library several times, which causes the above exception. I didnt checked this, but I also think `.DLL`-s could cause a problem, since windows file names are case-insensitive, while the code above is case-sensitive.

I don't know what would be the correct solution to this problem. I've currently patched cle to support both `dll` and `bpl`, but I'm not sure one could enumerate all the extensions for that check. 

What is the purpose of the check above? What would it break if removed, and object always have a provides field?

issue_title: Loading SEVIRI channel data which are not available in (native format) file
issue_body:
The MSG SEVIRI native format comes in one single file and may not include all channels.
Suppose the user try loading a channel which is not present in the file: At the moment the get_dataset method in the native_msg reader raises a KeyError. So, the issues is taken care of in principle, as it is possible to load other channels available in the file with the same scene instance. But, the KeyError handling is not propagated properly upwards in the code, resulting in a bit messy exception printouts, which is then repeated everytime a new channel is loaded.

As the HRIT data comes in segments and one file per channel this is not an issue there, but could potentially be an issue with other data with multiple channels in the same file.

We see two possible options:
1) Properly handle the KeyError upstream
2) When creating the scene object from the native format the header is read, and at that point missing and available datasets/channels are known. This information could be used before going into the channel loading. In that case the get_dataset would never need to return None or raise a KeyError.

Sauli Joro & Adam Dybbroe

issue_title: How one handle orphans data on the next client sync when permissions are removed for a user.
issue_body:
From discussion with @chrismbeckett on IRC:

3) Orphaned data in the client db when permissions change.

So you sync a bunch of data based on current permissions, and then another user changes the permissions so that you no longer have rights to that data. However, it is still sitting in their client side IndexDB and never gets nor updated nor removed.

issue_title: Custom admin site triggers exception
issue_body:
When using custom admin sites as described here:
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/contrib/admin/#customizing-the-adminsite-class

Certain bugs get triggered:

1) The site title/header/etc are not displayed, probably because Jet cannot find the attributes of the custom admin site.

2) When using custom views, a NoReverseMatch exception is raised.
The source of the problem seems to be in utils.py get_admin_site , which cannot find the custom admin site object. According to the Django documentation, this is solved by adding this to the custom view:
`request.current_app = "MyCustoAdmin"`
But this does not work with Jet.

issue_title: Entity Recognition Issue
issue_body:
Hello, first of all I appreciate tremendously to the Mycroft AI team for dedicating their passion towards opensource AI. I am learning and benefiting so much from this personally. 

I am not sure why this is happening but would like to ask if this is a bug internally in Adapt code for Python. 

In identical environmental situations (Other code/config same), with a command 

`"remind me at 5 pm to do something"` (after calling wake word)

if I have 
```python
reminder_keyword = [
'remind',
'remind me'
]
```

as reminder keyword entities, I get no intent that satisfies the query above. But then I tried, 
`"can you remind me at 5 pm to do something"` (after calling wake word)
Then it magically returns the correct reminder intent.

So feeling suspicious, I changed my reminder_keyword list to 
```python
reminder_keyword = [
'remind',
'remind me',
' remind ',
' remind me '
]
```
having same words with one that has no space, and another with spaces next to the phrase. 
Then as I suspected, it correctly picked up the intent from the same query that it failed before 
(`"remind me at 5 pm to do something"`). (nothing changed other than this)

Conclusion : 
Why is Adapt returning the correct intent when I added the phrases with spaces on each end?
I think this is a bug related to how Adapt handles spaces internally. For a better developing environment, I believe we shouldn't be required to add additional identical phrases with spaces at the end to make it work as intended. 

Thank you.

issue_title: Add support for testing a Pulp agent
issue_body:
An "agent" can be installed on a system. This agent allows Pulp to send commands to that system. See: http://pulp.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev-guide/architecture.html We should be able to test interactions between a consumer (a system with an agent) and a Pulp server.

Let's build out support for this scenario. One option is to change how we use the `settings.json` file. (The file is badly named. It should be named `server_configs.json`, or something like that.) Currently, that file may have a single "default" section. We can change the semantics assigned to that file and expect at least a "server" section, corresponding to a Pulp server, and optionally allow for the presence of a "consumer" section, corresponding to a system with the Pulp agent installed. Modules could then choose whether or not to run based on the presence or absence of this config file section. Here's a verbose example of what could be in a test module:

```python
def setUpModule():  # pylint:disable=invalid-name
    """Skip tests if there is no system running the Pulp agent."""
    set_up_module()
    if 'agent' not in config.ServerConfig().sections():
        raise unittest2.SkipTest(
            'This module requires a system running the Pulp agent.'
        )
```

Also see: https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/compare/master...Ichimonji10:test-agent

issue_title: confusing of adapt_param_noise in ddpg
issue_body:
Hi, I am little confusing about the schedule of doing ```adapt_param_noise``` at
https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/4993286230ac92ead39a66005b7042b56b8598b0/baselines/ddpg/training.py#L114

If seems the only time we adjust the noise scale is the condition at https://github.com/openai/baselines/blob/4993286230ac92ead39a66005b7042b56b8598b0/baselines/ddpg/training.py#L113 is satisfied. 
But the counter ```t``` is updated in the samples collection loop and used in the training loop. Thus, you could totally have a unlucky case, where your ```nb_rollout_steps``` is 51 and ```param_noise_adaption_interval``` is 50. So you can only update the noise scale every 50 cycle, which seems very strange.

issue_title: Unable to set description of enum values in graphene.Enum
String References not working with `InputFields`
issue_body:
In order to overcome some circular dependencies I switched over my Schema to use String References as mentioned in this issue: https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/issues/110

However, I've run into some issues with my `InputObjectType` classes.  So looking at something of the form:

```
# category_type.py
@schema.register
class CategoryInput(graphene.InputObjectType):
    Post = graphene.List('PostInput')

# post_type.py
@schema.register
class PostInput(graphene.InputObjectType):
    category = graphene.InputField('CategoryInput')
```

The `InputField` referencing 'CategoryInput' triggers an [AssertionError](https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/blob/master/graphql/type/definition.py#L618) saying that it's expecting an InputType as opposed to 'CategoryInput'.  However, the `List` seems to work correctly.  Taking a cursory glance at the code I'm thinking it's not correctly converting the String reference to the `InputObjectType`.

Any ideas?

issue_title: Improvements next stable package
issue_body:
Based on a new implementation, is needed to improve the following things for next stable package:

* [x] Schema lazy type registering: Register types as schema access it. 
* [x] Simplify schema base types. Avoid @memoize hacks. EDIT: Partially resolved in fields. Improve schema type getter
* [x] Add field description based on resolver function `__doc__`
* [x] Add python class `enums.Enum` mapping to a GraphQL Enum
* [x] Map received arguments keys by resolvers to be snake_case
* [x] Add Mutations
* [x] Add Relay Mutations
* [x] Generalize use of instance/types instantiation
* [ ] ~~Add field definition in types using only resolvers and decorators~~

One way for deal with most of this improvements is using a TypeRegister as epoxy is doing, however for new types behavior is not as easy to scale.

# Research on TypeRegister

Having a Type Register means it have to create a different object each time to have to access, for example, an interface (without doing any `_add_interface_impl` hacks).
Also, it means the `graphql-core` type is fixed for each defined class.

The advantages of having a fixed type `your_class.T` is that could be shared across not only this graphene projects, but any that uses graphql-core.
However, the cost of having a fixed type for each defined class are more than the advantages.

## Disadvantages

Why is not good to have a fixed type for each class? Some classes, like a relay `Node` depends a lot on the context where is being executed (or schema/registry). Why create a new `Node` class for just changing the context?
Also, it requires to add manually the type into the registry (via mixins, or hacking the registry for automatically adding my type).

## Practical solution

Create a `schema.T(object_type)` that will return the type for an object_type associated for this schema.

issue_title: Tornado in Python 2.7
issue_body:
I'm trying to run Graphql using Tornado Coroutines in Python 2.7. So I have created a new executor:

```
from tornado.gen import convert_yielded
from tornado.ioloop import IOLoop
from tornado.concurrent import is_future

from promise import Promise


class TornadoExecutor(object):
    def __init__(self, io_loop=None):
        if io_loop is None:
            io_loop = IOLoop.current()
        self.loop = io_loop
        self.future = None

    def wait_until_finished(self):
        # if there are futures to wait for
        while self.future is not None:
            # wait for the futures to finish
            future = self.future
            self.future = None
            self.loop.run_sync(lambda: future)

    def execute(self, fn, *args, **kwargs):
        result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
        if is_future(result):
            future = convert_yielded(result)
            if self.future:
                self.loop.add_future(self.future, lambda *_: None)
            self.future = future
            return Promise.resolve(future)
        return result
```

It only works if I create a new IOloop, like here:
https://gist.github.com/isi-gach/daef0b34ec5af6f026af52d593131c64

But I don't know how to make it work using the current IOloop, any ideas?

issue_title: Invalid Border Agent Locator in leader network data (unable to join)
issue_body:
I found that leader + commissioner can eventually go to an invalid state where Commissioner is sending keep alives and it is accepted by the leader but nobody can join.

The problem shows up when a border agent sent a leader petition in a child state (not leader, nor router). In that case a child RLOC is saved in network data Border Agent Locator.
When this thread device changes it's state to "router" its RLOC is changed but no-one updates this field in leader network data.

From this point a border agent sends LEAD_KA to the leader with commissioner session ID and gets valid response (Accept) but the border agent locator in the network data points to the device that doesn't exist.

I reproduced the issue using 3 ot_cli_ftd.
1. Run two clis and make sure that one is a leader and one is a child.
2. On the child cli run "commissioner start"
3. Make sure that it is still a child and verify netdatashow - this device RLOC should be found in network data. At this point any device can join as commissioner RLOC is the same as the one in network data.
4. Wait until commissioner changes state to router or force it by "state router"
5. Network data doesn't change and no other device can successfully join - never ...
This commissioner is sending LEAD_KA to the leader and gets Accept responses so that would mean that we have a valid commissioner and someone could join.

I couldn't find any related text in Thread docs but I think that leader could check border agent locator when he receives LEAD_KA.

issue_title: Stop using packman for Ruby gRPC client generation
issue_body:
Derived from #69:

Currently Ruby gRPC client pipeline use packman to generate gRPC files. However, that does not fit with the design of gcloud-ruby, where a package contains all of hand-written layers, Gapic-generated files, and gRPC-generated files.
That is Cloud APIs only, but there are no strong reasons to split packages for Gapic files and gRPC files in Ruby.

Because of that, we should stop using packman for Ruby's client pipeline. Instread, similar to Java, we should use protoc_tasks we already have.

Also, from https://github.com/googleapis/artman/issues/69#issuecomment-242172115,

> Currently we get protoc from linuxbrew here: https://github.com/grpc/homebrew-grpc
> That site indicates that the linuxbrew method is deprecated for all languages except C/C++. 

Therefore, Ruby will use grpc-tools rubygem (https://rubygems.org/gems/grpc-tools).

Cc: @bjwatson @geigerj

issue_title: Multi-threading on multiple epochs
issue_body:
The line 253 in fastext.cc receives one line from the stream at a time:

`localTokenCount += dict_->getLine(ifs, line, labels, model.rng);`

The different parts of the `ifs` are correctly distributed among the threads in the beginning (line 238 in `trainThread`). For one epoch I think it is OK, but when we do multiple epochs, the stream is being set to the beginning in line 258 of dictionary.cc when it reaches the end. If my reasoning is correct, this will lead to all the threads reading the same data (beginning of the file) in second epoch.

issue_title: Replacing pyparsing with shlex
issue_body:
Python's built-in "shlex" module for simple lexical analysis is probably good enough to handle our command-line parsing in cmd2 and it is implemented in C, so its performance would probably be a lot better than pyparsing, which is implemented in pure Python.

ANTLR and PLY also look like potentially superior alternatives to pyparsing, which is slow, dated, and at least in our case requires an overly-complicated grammar.

But shlex should be preferred since it is built-in to Python and not 3rd-party.

shlex:
https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/shlex.html

pyparsing:
http://pyparsing.wikispaces.com

ANTLR:
https://github.com/antlr/antlr4

PLY:
https://github.com/dabeaz/ply

This is a long-term "down the road" idea.

issue_title: Exponential growth in time required to run large numbers of tests
issue_body:
I'm still investigating the root issue, but it is reproducible.

I discovered this problem while working on https://github.com/tomcatmanager/tomcatmanager, a project which utilizes `cmd2`. A complete run of the test suite for that project makes several hundred calls to `cmd2.Cmd.onecmd_plus_hooks()`.  As the test suite has grown, I have noticed the performance of the test suite degrading in a non-linear way. I finally decided to try and track down why.

I discovered that as the number of calls to `cmd2.Cmd.onecmd_plus_hooks()` increases in a test run, the time required to execute each call grows very quickly. It's easy to duplicate using the test suite in `cmd2`. First, install the `pytest-repeat` module, which allows you to repeat each test in a suite any number of times.

Then run:
```
$ pytest tests/test_argparse.py -v --duration=0
```

On my computer those 18 tests run in less than 1 second. However, in the duration report at the end, which shows the clock time required for each test to run, you can see that some of the tests completed in 0.01 seconds, and others took 0.09 seconds. None of the tests in that file are an order of magnitude more expensive to run than the others, but you could maybe chalk it up to variation in what else is running on your computer.

Now try:
```
$ pytest tests/test_argparse.py -v --duration=0 --count=10
```
This will run each test 10 times. I expect that the time to run the tests would grow linearly with the number of iterations, so it should take perhaps 10 seconds to complete the run. Go ahead and go make some tea, because on my computer it took 464 seconds (almost 8 minutes!) to complete.

Examine the duration report at the end of the test run. Here's what I saw as the 10 slowest tests:
```
============================ slowest test durations ============================
9.28s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[8/10]
9.26s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[4/10]
9.20s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[10/10]
9.19s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[7/10]
9.17s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[9/10]
9.02s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[2/10]
8.78s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[1/10]
8.78s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[5/10]
8.76s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[3/10]
8.68s call     tests/test_argparse.py::test_subcommand_invalid_help[6/10]
```

Something has got to be broken, because there is no way it should take more than 9 seconds to run one of those tests.

When I made this discovery on https://github.com/tomcatmanager/tomcatmanager, I assumed there was some problem with the networking test code I had created, so I profiled the test runs and examined the output. The growth in clock time for the tests all occurs in the `pyparsing` module.

Using `pytest-xdist` seems to mask the problem, especially if you distribute the tests to a large number of workers. For example, if you use 16 workers, each worker will end up running approximately one sixteenth of the tests, and for "medium" sized test suites that would be enough to reduce the number of calls each worker makes to `pyparsing` so that the clock time required for each test doesn't begin to grow.

The `pytest-forked` module allows you to run each test in a new forked process. This prevents memory leaks or cache errors in one test from affecting other tests in the suite. If you install `pytest-forked` and run:
```
$ pytest tests/test_argparse.py -v --duration=0 --count=10 --forked
```

The test suite finishes 10 runs of each test (on my computer) in less than 9 seconds, which fits with the linear growth of test time I would expect.

At this point I think the likely culprit is the construction of the `cmd2` grammar for pyparsing, but it could also be a bug in pyparsing. More investigation definitely required. The good news is that this isn't super urgent, because there is a work around with `--forked`.

issue_title: verify_fingerprint doesn't work with self-signed certs
issue_body:
Using vdirsyncer 0.14.0 on Ubuntu 16.04, installed with pip3 install and Python 3.5.2.

Documentation should be updated to show that the 'verify_fingerprint' option doesn't work with self-signed certificates. The only option for that case is to copy the certificate and key from the server (using 'make-ssl-cert' on the server gave me a single file which worked fine with the 'verify' option in vdirsyncer.

It would be great if the 'verify_fingerprint' option could be modified/expanded so that self-signed certificates are allowed. This would require passing the 'verify=False' option to Python Requests, but then somehow still obtaining the SSL fingerprint and checking that it's right. I think that forcing the user of a server with a self-signed certificate to obtain the .crt file is a bit excessive (but maybe there's a good reason for that, which I didn't see explained anywhere).

Apart from this rather major stumbling block (many new users are likely to be using self-signed certificates, after all, because that's what you always start with), I found the software to be very nice and well designed.

issue_title: Certbot fails to retrieve domains of previous certificate when SAN is a critical extension
issue_body:
## My operating system is (include version):
CentOS 6 and CentOS 7

## I installed Certbot with (certbot-auto, OS package manager, pip, etc):
For CentOS6 (certbot-auto):  https://certbot.eff.org/all-instructions/#centos-6-none-of-the-above
For CentOS7 (yum):  https://certbot.eff.org/all-instructions/#centos-rhel-7-none-of-the-above

Currently using certbot 0.14.1 on CentOS 7 and certbot-auto 0.16.0 on CentOS 6.

## I ran this command and it produced this output:
certbot certonly --manual--cert-name cubs --domains chicago-cubs.venafi.example,cubs.venafi.example

_(note: non-Let's Encrypt ACME server specified in /etc/letsencrypt/cli.ini)_

```
Saving debug log to /var/log/letsencrypt/letsencrypt.log
Starting new HTTPS connection (1): tpp.venafi.example

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Confirm that you intend to update certificate cubs to include domains
[u'chicago-cubs.venafi.example', u'cubs.venafi.example']. Note that it
previously contained domains [].
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Certbot's behavior differed from what I expected because:
It appears that certbot is unable to parse domains from the previous certificate when it has no CN and the DNS SANs are a critical extension.  Symptom is the unexpected prompt shown above (applicable [previous_cert_pem.txt](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/files/1137352/previous_cert_pem.txt)).


## Background
Venafi has introduced an ACME server interface in Trust Protection Platform 17.2 and the common application involves using certbot to obtain certificates from an enterprise PKI.  Microsoft's Active Directory Certifiate Services (ADCS) is a very popular enterprise PKI and when it enrolls a certificate request that lacks a Subject DN (has only SANs) the subjectAltName (2.5.29.17) extension is always critical.  This behavior is consistent with [Section 4.2.1.6 of RFC 5280](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc5280#section-4.2.1.6) which states "If the subject field contains an empty sequence, then the issuing CA MUST include a subjectAltName extension that is marked as critical."

issue_title: Early crashes and custom error messages
issue_body:
With #4737, we still create a temporary log file without telling the user if we crash with one of our custom exception types before logging is configured. The reason for this is it was our previous behavior before #4444 of not mentioning logfiles if we knew why we crashed and tests like `tests/lock_test.py` currently rely on our behavior of creating the logfiles.

We should fix this, either by telling the user about the files or not creating them in this case, but I'm not sure it's necessary for 0.14.2 or that PR. I'm happy to implement either approach though.

issue_title: Erroneous `link %u down` message with sysid outputs
issue_body:
If sysid outputs cover all sysid's coming into MAVProxy, MAVProxy erroneously reports `link %u down`.
Cause is likely the early return from `master_callback()` in [mavproxy_link.py:286](https://github.com/ArduPilot/MAVProxy/blob/7716afd78c0e5fafec9d7b1df3c3c62a611fb302/MAVProxy/modules/mavproxy_link.py#L286). This early return prevents the master's `last_message` time from being updated when all messages are handled by sysid outputs. This results in the timeout that causes the `link %u down`.

Rather than having sysid outputs exclusively handle messages for a specific sysid, could the sysid outputs instead receive a copy of the message? The message can then continue to be processed as normal, being sent over non-sysid outputs too. This was the behaviour I expected initially.

May be impacted by changes in issues #443 and #444

issue_title: Option to remove descriptive elements
issue_body:
Following up on #35, I found that `<title>` and `<desc>` (as well as `<metadata>`) are purely descriptive elements, according to the [SVG 1.1 specification](http://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/intro.html#TermDescriptiveElement). There already is an option to remove `<metadata>` (`--remove-metadata`), but not for `<title>` and `<desc>`.

What we could do:
1.  Add `<title>` and `<desc>` to the same option.
2.  Add a new option that removes them (either separately or together).

Any thoughts?

issue_title: Somewhat effective way of using values()
issue_body:
I wonder if you might want to document this alternative when using values()?  Even though this isn't really django-polymorphic specific, although I think you're the one adding in the ctype.

In this example, we have A->B->C and A->D->E

E has a column 'foo', and C has a column 'bar'.

This works by joining in the model name from the ctype.  Then also joining in the specific attributes of the inherited models ...

 A.objects.values('polymorphic_ctype__model', 'b__c__foo', 'd__e__bar')

One could look at the model string returned instead of isinstance and then select which fields to look at in the code.  The irrelevant ones end up being None.

...maybe this is obvious, but it was kind of an Aha! moment for me when trying to convert some queries to values().

issue_title: not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 1)
issue_body:
Any command in a Bash cell produces the expected standard output, but then prints `not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 1)`.
Following that, execution of any other cell types (including `sos`) starts generating that same message, until the `sos` kernel is restarted. Variable assignments in `sos` cells no longer work after the Bash error message. Until a Bash cell is executed, Bash magic `!` in `sos` cells works OK, as well as Sos, R, JavaScript and Python3 cell types.
The installation is on CentOS 6.5 with the latest Miniconda3, 64 bit. Tried both installing into a separate Sos environment, as well as into Conda root environment. Tried both the release and Git master head.

issue_title: Behavior of %with when calling from a non-SoS kernel.
issue_body:
I believe the current implementation assumes `SoS` as the current kernel so 

```
%with R -i var1 -o var2
```
sends `var` to `R` before switching and returns `var2` to `SoS` after completion of the cell.

Now, if we are in another kernel (e.g. `Matlab` as the default kernel), we should send `var1` in **Matlab** to `R` and return `var2` back to `Matlab`.

issue_title: Broken with Tox 3.2.0
issue_body:
Tox 3.2.0+ with tox-pip-extensions 1.2.1 is broken due to tox-dev/tox#935 changing the default `install_command` and this having a [hardcoded](https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-pip-extensions/blob/master/tox_pip_extensions.py#L27) value.

Either the check needs to take this into account in the comparison at [line 65](https://github.com/tox-dev/tox-pip-extensions/blob/master/tox_pip_extensions.py#L65) or compare directly with `tox.config.InstallcmdOption.default`.

Also, it might be nice if it raised an exception instead of calling `sys.exit` or otherwise gave some context; it was a little mystifying at first to receive this error from seemingly out of nowhere:

```
!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
tox-pip-extension(s) (venv_update) used but testenv (py3) sets install_command!
!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
```

issue_title: Warning message that worker does not have function registered is usually unwarranted.
issue_body:
Pretty much every time I've seen this error message, it has not been relevant. It can be triggered by bad timing.

```
This worker was asked to execute a function that it does not have registered. You may have to restart Ray.

You can inspect errors by running

    ray.error_info()

If this driver is hanging, start a new one with

    ray.init(redis_address="123.45.67.89:6379")
```

For example, suppose export a ton of remote functions and then call the thousandth one. The task gets scheduled on some worker. But that worker is still busy importing all 1000 remote functions (and unpickling them). If five seconds pass and the worker hasn't gotten to the 1000th remote function yet, then it will print this warning. However, eventually it should receive the function and execute the task, so there isn't really a problem.

We should consider increasing the timeout or solving this in a different way.

issue_title: [xray] perf regression when transferring a large number of small objects across dockers
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**:
Ubuntu in docker
Linux f3559e14813a 4.9.87-linuxkit-aufs #1 SMP Wed Mar 14 15:12:16 UTC 2018 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux

- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: source
- **Ray version**: 0.5.0
- **Python version**: Python 2.7.12 :: Anaconda 4.2.0 (64-bit)
- **Exact command to reproduce**:   See below for details

### Describe the problem

I wrote a python script for performance test, which transfers 100K integers from on actor to another actor, each actor running in a different docker.  Previously with legacy ray it takes about 1 minute, while with raylet enabled it takes 3 minutes. 

On docker 2, do:

# ray start --head --redis-port=6379 --resources='{"Resource2": 4}'  --use-raylet

On docker1, do:

# ray start --redis-address 172.17.0.3:6379 --resources='{"Resource1": 2}' --use-raylet

Then on docker1, run:

# python perf.py

### Source code / logs

[Perf.py source code]

from __future__ import absolute_import
from __future__ import division
from __future__ import print_function

import ray
import time
import datetime

@ray.remote(resources={'Resource1': 2})
class A(object):
    def __init__(self):
        print("Actor A start...")

    def f(self):
        num_of_items = 100 * 1000

        b = B.remote(num_of_items)
        time_str = datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S:%f')
        print("push, start " + time_str)
        for i in range(num_of_items):
            b.f.remote(i)
        time_str = datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S:%f')
        print("push, end " + time_str)
        time.sleep(5)

@ray.remote(resources={'Resource2': 4})
class B(object):
    def __init__(self, num_of_items):
        print("Actor B start...")
        self.num_of_items = num_of_items
        self.sum = 0

    def f(self, object_id):
        self.sum += 1
        if (self.sum == 1) :
            time_str = datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S:%f')
            print("read, start " + time_str)

        if (self.sum == self.num_of_items) :
            time_str = datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S:%f')
            print("read, end " + time_str)

    def get_sum(self):
        return self.sum

if __name__ == "__main__":
    ray.init(redis_address="172.17.0.3:6379",use_raylet=True)
    A.remote().f.remote()
    time.sleep(300)

issue_body:
- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [ ] This is a plugin request.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] I used the search function to find already opened/closed issues or pull requests.

### Description
I've been browsing the information on the configuration pages, but I can't find a way to achieve something I want to do. I would like to have the ability to save streams I frequently watch and reload them with a simpler command, like "streamlink one" for instance. It would save users a lot of time, since longer/weirder links can't be memorized easily and have to be sought out or kept as a bookmark. On lower-spec PCs running Linux, opening a browser and waiting for a bookmark to load can require a lot of time and CPU usage. 

Excuse me if there's already a way of doing this, I was unable to find it.

As suggested in #261, we should add an [issue template] (https://help.github.com/articles/creating-an-issue-template-for-your-repository/) to the repo to highlight known issues, common problems and any information that should be provided when creating an issue. For example, which OS and version of OS they are using, which version of Streamlink they are using (and maybe to try the nightly), how they installed Streamlink, which URLs they tried, and any error given on the console. Any thing else? 

I realise that some of that will overlap the contribution guidelines, but I don't see that as a problem.

issue_title: Add a fallback mechanism
issue_body:
Right now `LocalizedValue` falls back to the primary language if no translation for the current language is present. I think it's interesting if the user could specify a fallback chain. This could be in a per-missing-language fashion:

```
LOCALIZED_FIELDS_FALLBACKS = {
    'en': ['es', 'eu'],
    'es': ['eu', 'en'],
    'eu': ['es', 'en'],
}
```

For backwards compatibility, `LOCALIZED_FIELDS_FALLBACKS.get(lang, [PRIMARY_LANGUAGE])`.

This could be a global setting or a field parameter.

issue_title: Unintended object mutation
issue_body:
in caldav/davclient.py:
DAVClient.request lines 221-222:
        combined_headers = self.headers
        combined_headers.update(headers)
First line makes combined_headers to be a *reference* to self.headers, and therefore second line, in fact, updates self.headers with values from headers, affecting all subsequent requests.
The combined_headers should be a copy instead:
         combined_headers = dict(self.headers)
         combined_headers.update(headers)

issue_title: observe() in a loop doesn't work properly
issue_body:
The following code has 4 widgets. The first two are generated by a loop and don't work properly. When you click the first widget, it claims you clicked the second widget . The last two widgets work fine

```
import ipywidgets as widgets
from IPython.display import Image, display, clear_output

# a and b in loop
def wi_do(title, change):
    print(title, change, "\n")
for title in ['a', 'b']:
    wi = widgets.SelectMultiple(
                options= ['one', 'two'],
                value= ['one'],
                description=title
            )
    display(wi)
    wi.observe(lambda x: wi_do(title, x), names='value')
    
# A
A = widgets.SelectMultiple(
            options= ['one', 'two'],
            value= ['one'],
            description='A'
        )
display(A)
def A_do(change):
    print(A.description, change, "\n")
A.observe(A_do, names='value')

# B
B = widgets.SelectMultiple(
            options= ['one', 'two'],
            value= ['one'],
            description='B'
        )
display(B)
def B_do(change):
    print(B.description, change, "\n")
B.observe(B_do, names='value')
```

issue_title: widgets and access from different browsers.
issue_body:
Hi,

first of all, thanks again for this code :)

I am having a strange problem with the widgets, which could also as well be the intended behavior, but I thought I would ask here.

My use case is that I am using the widgets (Toggles, Accordions, Tabs, interactive) to present an overview of the results of a notebook. The notebook is left running on a jupyter server. Users connect to the server and open the running window, where all code cells have been hidden. They are presented with something very similar to a dashboard, with some interactivity provided by the widgets.

Everything works fine as long as I only access the page from the same browser. If I change browser (even when running on the same machine), the widgets stop working as expected. The actual effect depends on the situation, the most common being that an interactive view is actually displayed several times, and it is not interactive anymore. Toggle/observe callbacks also often appear to be broken. The only cure is to rerun the cells that generated the widgets.

I am not talking about concurrent simultaneous access by multiple users. It is always just me. I close one browser and re-open the same notebook on a second one. I am guessing that the status of the widgets is somehow being stored part on the server and part on the browser, so that a new browser finds an incomplete/partial snapshot that it cannot handle properly. Is this what is happening? Is this the expected behavior? Is there anything I can do to remove/improve this weird behavior?

Thanks for your help,

Andrea.

issue_title: `repr` call after `AppException` instantiation raises `AttributeError`
issue_body:
The custom `str` method of `AppException` just reproduces the base class `str` method. The only thing the custom `repr` does is change the format, but I think we should consider sticking to the baseclass version for improved readability (it matches the built-in format); additionally, it introduces a bug because if `reason` is not set, the `repr` call will fail with an `AttributeError`.
```
In [28]: from parsl.app.errors import ParslError, AppException

In [29]: x = ParslError('baz')

In [30]: y = AppException('baz')

In [31]: x
Out[31]: parsl.app.errors.ParslError('baz')

In [32]: y
Out[32]: parsl.app.errors.AppException('baz')

In [33]: repr(x)
Out[33]: "ParslError('baz',)"

In [34]: repr(y)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AttributeError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-34-2a48cbef3bc0> in <module>()
----> 1 repr(y)

~/ci/parsl/parsl/app/errors.py in __repr__(self)
     40
     41     def __repr__(self):
---> 42         return "{0} Reason:{1}".format(self.__class__, self.reason)
     43
     44     def __str__(self):

AttributeError: 'AppException' object has no attribute 'reason'

In [35]: y.reason = 'baz'

In [36]: repr(y)
Out[36]: "<class 'parsl.app.errors.AppException'> Reason:baz"
```

issue_title: CoreV1Api read_namespaced_service with argument export=True returns immutable fields
issue_body:
According to the example for CoreV1Api [`read_namespaced_service`](https://github.com/kubernetes-client/python/blob/master/kubernetes/docs/CoreV1Api.md#read_namespaced_service), when `export=True` the returned kubernetes object should not contain "fields that a user can not specify". I assume this to mean that only mutable fields will be returned.

In my particular case, my program is building a new kubernetes service object, and then calling `create_namespaced_service`. If that results with an `ApiException` due to the object already existing, the program will then try to patch the existing kubernetes service object by generating a json patch following [RFC 6902](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6902) describing the changes between the existing service object and the newly created service object.

For example, I have a kubernetes service object built from the ground up by my program. Its sanitized JSON representation looks like this:

```
{'apiVersion': 'v1',
 'kind': 'Service',
 'metadata': {'labels': {'name': 'name123'}, 'name': 'name123'},
 'spec': {'ports': [{'name': 'agshttp', 'port': 6080, 'targetPort': 'agshttp'},
                    {'name': 'agshttps',
                     'port': 6443,
                     'targetPort': 'agshttps'}],
          'selector': {'name': 'name123'}}}
```

An **identical** service with the same name already exists (generated by the same program in a previous run), so the call to `create_namespaced_service` fails as expected. The program then calls `read_namespaced_service` with `export=True` to get the following sanitized JSON representation:

```
{'apiVersion': 'v1',
 'kind': 'Service',
 'metadata': {'labels': {'name': 'name123'},
              'name': 'name123',
              'selfLink': '/api/v1/namespaces//services/name123'},
 'spec': {'clusterIP': '<unknown>',
          'ports': [{'name': 'agshttp',
                     'port': 6080,
                     'protocol': 'TCP',
                     'targetPort': 'agshttp'},
                    {'name': 'agshttps',
                     'port': 6443,
                     'protocol': 'TCP',
                     'targetPort': 'agshttps'}],
          'selector': {'name': 'name123'},
          'sessionAffinity': 'None',
          'type': 'ClusterIP'},
 'status': {'loadBalancer': {}}}
```

The JSON patch describing the changes looks like this:

```
[{'op': 'remove', 'path': '/status'},
 {'op': 'remove', 'path': '/metadata/selfLink'},
 {'op': 'remove', 'path': '/spec/sessionAffinity'},
 {'op': 'remove', 'path': '/spec/type'},
 {'op': 'remove', 'path': '/spec/clusterIP'},
 {'op': 'remove', 'path': '/spec/ports/0/protocol'},
 {'op': 'remove', 'path': '/spec/ports/1/protocol'}]
```

The program then feeds the json patch into `patch_namespaced_service`, and results in the following error:

```
kubernetes.client.rest.ApiException: (500)
Reason: Internal Server Error
HTTP response headers: HTTPHeaderDict({'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'Date': 'Fri, 02 Feb 2018 08:05:07 GMT', 'Content-Length': '135'})
HTTP response body: {"kind":"Status","apiVersion":"v1","metadata":{},"status":"Failure","message":"Unable to remove nonexistent key: selfLink","code":500}
```

I know from debugging the problem that the operations for removing `/metadata/selfLink` and `/spec/clusterIP` are to blame. I was able to get the patch to work after removing those specific operations in a debugging session. **Shouldn't these fields be excluded when calling `read_namespaced_service` with `export=True`?**

I'm not sure if this is a Python client library issue, a kubernetes issue, or a total misunderstanding on my part. Any help would be appreciated.

My program uses the same methodology for creating/patching deployments, horizontal pod autoscalers, and ingresses. Those seem to work fine. My program is using the kubernetes==4.0.0 Python client, and my server version of kubernetes is 1.6

issue_title: S3 GET Response header Content-Type should be 'binary/octet-stream' not 'text/html'
issue_body:
If you make a GET request to URL for a file that is publicly readable, the content-type is being set to `text/html`, but when you do the same for the real S3, the content-type is set to `binary/octet-stream`.  This allows the browser to know to just download it as a file, instead of trying to display it.

## To Reproduce:

```python
import boto3
import os
import requests
from botocore.config import Config
from StringIO import StringIO

host = 'localhost'
port = 4572
s3 = boto3.resource('s3',
 config=Config(s3={'addressing_style': 'path'}),
 endpoint_url="http://{}:{}".format(host, port)
)

s3.create_bucket(Bucket='test-bucket')
bucket = s3.Bucket('test-bucket')

key = 'blah.txt'
file_obj = StringIO('hello world')
bucket.upload_fileobj(file_obj, key, ExtraArgs={"ACL": 'public-read'})
url = s3.meta.client.generate_presigned_url(
    'get_object', ExpiresIn=0, Params={'Bucket': bucket.name, 'Key': key}
)
url = url.split('?')[0]

response = requests.get(url)
print response.headers['content-type']
```

When running this against AWS S3, the content-type for txt, pdf, csv and html filetype extensions is `binary/octet-stream`.    When running the same thing but connected to localstack, I always get `text/html; charset=utf-8`.

issue_title: default API version ignored
issue_body:
The example config file [here](http://python-gitlab.readthedocs.io/en/stable/cli.html#cli) shows that `api_version` can go in the `global` section, so I have configured like this

```
[global]
default = prod
ssl_verify = True
timeout = 5
api_version = 4

[prod]
url = https://git.example.com
private_token = blahblah 

[stage]
url = https://git.stage.example.com
private_token = blahblah
```

Running with `--debug` I see it making v3 calls

```
$ gitlab -o json --debug project get --id 1
send: b'GET /api/v3/projects/1 HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: git.example.com\r\nUser-Agent: python-requests/2.18.4\r\nAccept-Encoding: gzip, deflate\r\nAccept: */*\r\nConnection: keep-alive\r\nPRIVATE-TOKEN: blahblah\r\n\r\n'
reply: 'HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n'
DEBUG:urllib3.connectionpool:https://git.example.com:443 "GET /api/v3/projects/1 HTTP/1.1" 200 1542
header: Server header: Date header: Content-Type header: Content-Length header: Connection header: Cache-Control header: Etag header: Vary header: X-Frame-Options header: X-Request-Id header: X-Runtime header: Strict-Transport-Security id: 1
path: scratch
```

Adding an explicit `api_version`  to each server resolves this.

issue_title: Specifing input shapes example
issue_body:
When converting models from Core ML, the batch size is unknown (variable-length) by default. To overwrite this setting, one can specify their own input shapes.

Consider MNIST.mlmodel downloaded at `https://github.com/likedan/Awesome-CoreML-Models`. We can set batch size to 3 by running the following conversion code.

```python
import onnx
from onnxmltools.utils import visualize_model
from onnxmltools.convert.coreml.convert import convert
from onnxmltools.convert.common.data_types import FloatTensorType
from winmltools.utils import save_model
from winmltools.utils import save_text
from coremltools.models.utils import load_spec

coreml_model = load_spec('MNIST.mlmodel')

# Each input type is a tuple of (variable_name, variable_type). To find Core ML variable names
# and their types, you can print out coreml_model.description. If the considered Core ML
# variable, say coreml_model.description.input[0], is an image, you need to find out its color
# space value via printing coreml_model.description.input[0].type.imageType.colorSpace. 
# If the value of colorSpace is 10/20/30, the corresponding color space is 'GRAY'/'RGB'/'BGR'.
initial_type = (coreml_model.description.input[0].name, FloatTensorType(shape=[1, 1, 28, 28], color_space='GRAY')) # Other allowed color spaces are "RGB" and "BGR"
onnx_model = convert(coreml_model, initial_types=[initial_type])
# The produced ONNX model in text format
save_text(onnx_model, "mnist.onnx.txt")
# The produced ONNX model
save_model(onnx_model, 'mnist.onnx')
# Call a simple visualization tool
visualize_model(onnx_model)
```

issue_title: Remove dependency on clldutils
issue_body:
Functionality of `clldutils` is still used in some places. These should be removed such that csvw 1.0 does not depend on `clldutils`:
- [ ] `clldutils.attrlib`: `asdict` and `valid_re` should be copied here.
- [ ] `clldutils.misc`: `lazyproperty` should be copied here, `normalize_name` should be moved here, i.e. be reomoved from `clldutils` as of v2.0
- [ ] `clldutils.path.move`: should be copied here.

issue_title: Many scripts do use journalctl output in live mode which takes time
issue_body:
**What are you missing in citellus?**

As journalctl in offline mode is defined based on sosreport, for 'live' mode generate once to a temporary file, prefill on first execution or if empty, and then handle that file as journalctl variable like what we do in sosreport mode but for live mode.

This will require to cleanup some of the scripts that use it and use the var instead but will be faster in live exection

**Additional info**

issue_title: Allow specification of fields in Search
issue_body:
The search class is a very helpful thing to find id's of what you're looking for, but I often feel like searching for just a string is a handicap. Allowing the specification of fields would make searching a lot more powerful.

For example:

```python
search = Search('baby one more time', artist = 'britney spears')
```

The above Search object could return albums that feature a song with the name of the first argument or an album, single, EP or whatever with that name, while keeping the artist limitation (which might be a search in itself) in mind.
